does: This paper introduces MENLO, a framework for evaluating whether large language models produce native-like quality responses across 47 languages and dialects. The authors create a dataset of 6,423 human-annotated preference pairs across four quality dimensions (clarity, naturalness, informativeness, and safety) grounded in audience design theory. They systematically evaluate zero-shot LLM judges, finding that pairwise comparison and structured rubrics improve performance but still fall short of human annotators. The team then fine-tunes LLM judges using reinforcement learning, reward shaping, and multi-task learning, showing substantial gains. They further demonstrate that RL-trained judges can function as generative reward models to improve multilingual LLM outputs, though gaps with human judgment persist.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in evaluation metrics for natural language generation, extending from BLEU and ROUGE to more sophisticated learned preference models. It responds to recent advances in using LLMs as evaluators and judges, following work that showed language models can assess quality comparably to humans in English. The paper engages with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward modeling techniques developed for alignment. It also connects to emerging research on multilingual NLP evaluation, which has lagged far behind English-focused benchmarking. The audience design framework draws from sociolinguistics literature on context-sensitive communication. The preference pair annotation methodology extends work in pairwise comparison datasets like Anthropic's Constitutional AI and similar preference collection efforts.
enables: This framework enables scalable evaluation of multilingual LLM quality without requiring human annotators for every new model or language variant. The RL-trained judges could be deployed to automatically evaluate and filter model outputs across dozens of languages, reducing evaluation bottlenecks in model development. The findings suggest pathways for preference alignment in truly multilingual settings, where current RLHF approaches remain primarily English-centric. Release of the annotated dataset provides a foundation for training culturally-aware evaluators and studying what makes responses feel native-like across different language communities. Future work could extend this to low-resource languages, investigate which quality dimensions are most culturally variable, and develop better methods for cross-lingual transfer of evaluation criteria, ultimately supporting development of LLMs that perform equitably across the world's languages.
